items.evidence_text:
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it simple and practical. Going back to the month-to-month piece first—what’s your income these days? Like, what’s coming in over a year, roughly.
Client: It’s my pension, paid weekly. If I’m remembering right it’s about a hundred and fourteen thousand a year.
Advisor: Okay, about $114,000 a year gross from the pension, paid weekly—got it.
Client: Yes, gross. Not what hits my checking.
Advisor: Good catch—gross versus net. When you say “not what hits checking,” you mean taxes and maybe health premiums come out before you see it?

Client: I’d say around three thousand a month.
Advisor: Okay, around $3,000. Is that including everything—like property taxes, insurance, groceries, medical, the whole thing?
Client: Uh… maybe not. Hang on—no, with everything it’s more like $3,250 a month.
Advisor: Great, thank you for double-checking. So $3,250 per month total household expenses.
Client: Yes, that’s closer.

Advisor: And then separate from that, any debt payments each month? Credit cards, loans—anything like that.
Client: Just that loan. It’s about four hundred a month.
Advisor: Okay, $400 a month. When you say “loan,” is it like a personal loan, or is it more like a credit card balance?
Client: It’s a loan—well, it feels like a credit card because the rate is awful.
Advisor: Got it. So it’s structured as a loan but high interest. Do you know the balance roughly?

Advisor: Great. So primary residence about $291,900.
Client: Yes.
Advisor: Quick check—earlier you said investable was around $340k to $350k. If I add the taxable $143,450, retirement $105,450, bank $50,550, and private markets $47,050, that comes to $346,450. Does that match what you have in mind?
Client: Yes—$346,450. That’s the number I couldn’t quite pull up at first.
Advisor: Perfect. Going back to the loan for a second—just to make sure I didn’t misunderstand—your monthly debt payment is $400, separate from the $3,250 of regular expenses, right?

Client: Mm-hm.
Advisor: And then your home—just so we have the full picture—what do you think the house is worth these days?
Client: Around $292,000. I mean, $291,900 is what I wrote down from the last estimate.
Advisor: Great. So primary residence about $291,900.
Client: Yes.

Client: Yes—$346,450. That’s the number I couldn’t quite pull up at first.
Advisor: Perfect. Going back to the loan for a second—just to make sure I didn’t misunderstand—your monthly debt payment is $400, separate from the $3,250 of regular expenses, right?
Client: Right. Separate. And the balance is $4,600, not five thousand—I rounded too much earlier.
Advisor: Thank you—$4,600 outstanding, $400 a month, about 24.49% interest, and it runs to October 2032 if left as-is.
Client: Yes, that’s it.

Advisor: Perfect. And just so I’m not assuming—do you spend most of the year there, or do you do the snowbird thing?
Client: No, no, I’m there all year.
Advisor: Okay. One quick admin-y thing: the type of firm you’re working with here—this is a U.S. RIA setup. Does that sound like what you were expecting, Margaret?
Client: I think so? I’m not totally sure what that means.
Advisor: Totally fair. It just means a U.S.-based registered investment advisor—advice-first, not like an insurance-only shop. Does that match what you thought you were coming in for?

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Margaret. Going back to something you said earlier about keeping the education money kind of separate in your head—before we get there, I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right.
Client: Sure, yeah.
Advisor: First one’s simple: marital status. You’re widowed, correct, Margaret?
Client: Yes, widowed.
Advisor: Thanks. And household size—when I say “adults in the household,” I mean people living with you who are adults. Is it just you, one adult, or is someone else living there too?

Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it simple and practical. Going back to the month-to-month piece first—what’s your income these days? Like, what’s coming in over a year, roughly.
Client: It’s my pension, paid weekly. If I’m remembering right it’s about a hundred and fourteen thousand a year.

Client: Yes, gross. Not what hits my checking.
Advisor: Good catch—gross versus net. When you say “not what hits checking,” you mean taxes and maybe health premiums come out before you see it?

Advisor: Okay, one adult. I’m going to pause you there—because I’ve seen this get weird—when you say “just me,” you’re not counting the grandkids because they’re not adults, right?
Client: Right, not counting them. Just me.
Advisor: Got it. And what state are you a resident of for taxes—New Jersey?
Client: Yes, New Jersey.
Advisor: Perfect. And just so I’m not assuming—do you spend most of the year there, or do you do the snowbird thing?

Client: Mainly that I’m not going to run short month to month, and then… the education money for the grandkids. And I guess that loan, which I hate talking about.

Advisor: Now, risk—when we say “risk tolerance,” we’re talking about how you want the investments positioned overall. Would you say you’re more conservative, moderate, or growth-oriented?
Client: I’d say growth. I mean, not reckless, but growth.
Advisor: Growth—thank you. And just so I don’t mix terms: growth doesn’t mean “all stocks all the time,” it’s more the overall tilt. Does that match how you think about it?

Client: I’d say around three thousand a month.

Advisor: Okay—when you say 22%, are you thinking of the bracket, or maybe what you see withheld from the pension checks?
Client: Probably the withholding. Sorry—yeah, that’s what I see coming out.
Advisor: No worries at all—that’s a super common mix-up. The bracket we have on file is 32%. Does 32% sound more like the bracket you’re in?
Client: Yes—32% is the bracket. The 22% was just what I notice coming out weekly.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you for correcting that.

Client: I think so, yes—gross before taxes.
Advisor: Okay, good. And you’re still fully retired, correct? No part-time work tucked in anywhere?
Client: Yes, I’m retired. No job income.
Advisor: Got it. One sec—let me jot that down.

Advisor: Got it. One sec—let me jot that down.

Client: It’s August 3rd, 1943.
Advisor: August 3rd, 1943—thank you.
Advisor: And just to be clear, that makes you—hang on—82?

Client: No—83. I turned 83 last August.
Advisor: Right, sorry—83. Thank you for correcting me.
Advisor: Quick admin thing: in my notes I label people as Client 1, Client 2, that kind of thing. Since it’s just you, Margaret, you’re Client 1—does that sound right?
Client: Actually—no, I think you have me as Client 0 in your system. It’s weird, but I’ve seen it on a statement.
Advisor: Oh—okay, thanks for catching that. So you’re labeled Client 0 on the paperwork you’ve seen?
